coredata.dc:title: Tunable photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots synthesized via laser ablation in ethanol

coredata.dc:description: GQDs were synthesized via eco-friendly pulsed laser ablation in the liquid (PLAL) method, employing ethanol as the solvent and carbon black as the carbon source. The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of GQDs was systematically analyzed using emission-excitation matrices across varying concentrations and pH conditions. Increasing GQD concentrations resulted in redshifts of maximum PL excitation bands, transitioning from 235 nm to 272 nm and subsequently to 365 nm. The corresponding PL emission shifted from 310 nm to 410 nm and finally to 495 nm. These findings reveal aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) in the blue region and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) at longer wavelengths, emphasizing the role of aggregation in modulating GQD PL properties. The photoluminescence of GQDs was also strongly influenced by pH. At acidic pH, dual emission maxima were observed at 375 nm and 450 nm, linked to higher aggregation states due to the protonation of edge groups. Conversely, at pH values above 7.0, the GQDs exhibited a single emission peak at 470 nm, reflecting controlled aggregation dominated by core group contributions. Neutral pH yielded a redshifted emission maximum at 460 nm, attributed to oxygenated groups in the core structure. This work elucidates the crucial influence of aggregation and pH on GQD photoluminescence, offering essential insights into their mechanisms when synthesized without acidic agents. Adopting a sustainable synthesis approach reveals the tunable luminescent properties of high-purity, undoped GQDs, advancing their potential for environmentally friendly applications and supporting the design of graphene-based nanomaterials for targeted functionalities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM analyses of the GQDs generated by PLAL, along with a histogram showing a Gaussian distribution and a narrow size variation of the GQDs, (b) UV–Vis analyses ranging from 10 to 300 μg mL−1, (c) Raman analyses of the GQDs in powder form, with a focus on the D and G bands, were conducted and (d) FTIR analyses were conducted with emphasis on the functional groups and types of oxygenated functions, highlighted by color and region in the spectrum. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
XPS analysis of GQDs (a) survey XPS spectra, (b) high-resolution C1s, and (c) high-resolution O1s spectra.

(a), (b), (c) Maximum PL emission and corresponding excitation spectra of A-GQDs, B-GQDs, and C-GQDs with increasing concentration, respectively. (d), (e) and (f 2D PL excitation and emission spectra of A-GQDs, B-GQDs, and C-GQDs at different wavelengths with increasing concentration, respectively.

(a) Excitation curves showing characteristic peaks of edge and core effects of GQDs for emission at 470 nm and (b) emission curves corresponding to aggregation-induced effects with increasing concentration at three different wavelengths.

2D photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs in a 0.05 mol L−1 Britton-Robinson buffer, illustrating the variation in maximum fluorescence emission at pH values: (a) 2.0, (b) 3.0, (c) 4.0, (d) 5.0, (e) 6.0, (f) 7.0, (g) 8.0, (h) 9.0, and (i) 10.0.

(a), (b), (c) Maximum PL emission and corresponding excitation spectra of GQDs in HCl 1 mol L−1, GQDs in ultrapure water, and GQDs NaOH 1 mol L−1, respectively. (d), (e) and (f) 2D PL excitation and emission spectra for GQDs in HCl 1.0 mol L−1, GQDs in ultrapure water, and GQDs NaOH 1.0 mol L−1, respectively.

Illustration of proposed aggregation mechanisms for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) under varying concentrations in acidic, neutral, and basic media. Arrows depict interactions between GQDs, while colors represent pH-dependent behaviors: blue indicates lateral interactions between edge groups, yellow signifies planar interactions arising from core interactions, and green reflects the equilibrium between the two interaction types. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Distinct roles of edge functional groups vs. core structure.

row.entry: Governed by –OH/–COOH protonation/deprotonation; C=O enhances electron withdrawal.
Insensitive to pH; fluorescence depends on quantum confinement and dispersion.
–COOH/–OH protonation reduces surface charge; C=O mediates hydrogen bonding.
Drives π-π stacking when edge-group repulsion is lost.
–OH/–COO- modulate bandgap via electron withdrawal; C=O stabilizes excited states.
Intrinsic emission from sp2 domains; quenched by aggregation.
XPS confirms the dominance of –OH (62.5 %) and C=O/–COOH (37.5 %), critical for pH response.
XPS confirms sp2 graphitic structure; PL spectra reflect confinement.

head.ce:title: Tunable photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots synthesized via laser ablation in ethanol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs were synthesized via eco-friendly pulsed laser ablation in the liquid (PLAL) method, employing ethanol as the solvent and carbon black as the carbon source. The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of GQDs was systematically analyzed using emission-excitation matrices across varying concentrations and pH conditions. Increasing GQD concentrations resulted in redshifts of maximum PL excitation bands, transitioning from 235 nm to 272 nm and subsequently to 365 nm. The corresponding PL emission shifted from 310 nm to 410 nm and finally to 495 nm. These findings reveal aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) in the blue region and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) at longer wavelengths, emphasizing the role of aggregation in modulating GQD PL properties. The photoluminescence of GQDs was also strongly influenced by pH. At acidic pH, dual emission maxima were observed at 375 nm and 450 nm, linked to higher aggregation states due to the protonation of edge groups. Conversely, at pH values above 7.0, the GQDs exhibited a single emission peak at 470 nm, reflecting controlled aggregation dominated by core group contributions. Neutral pH yielded a redshifted emission maximum at 460 nm, attributed to oxygenated groups in the core structure. This work elucidates the crucial influence of aggregation and pH on GQD photoluminescence, offering essential insights into their mechanisms when synthesized without acidic agents. Adopting a sustainable synthesis approach reveals the tunable luminescent properties of high-purity, undoped GQDs, advancing their potential for environmentally friendly applications and supporting the design of graphene-based nanomaterials for targeted functionalities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been gaining increasing attention due to their photoluminescence properties [1], as well as their high water solubility [2] and low toxicity [3] which makes them potential candidates to replace commonly used semiconductor nanomaterials based on cadmium (Cd), which is known to be toxic [4]. In addition, they have diverse applications, such as in solar cells [5], sensors [6], catalysis [7], bioimaging [8], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [9], chemical carriers [10], and many others.

Currently, the methods employed for the fabrication of GQDs can be of the top-down type, where a bulk material, which can be a material with a large number of graphene sheets such as graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, carbon black, graphite powder, coal, and biomass, is physically destroyed to generate smaller sheets with quantum confinement properties [11–13]. Alternatively, bottom-up approaches can be employed, where molecular precursors like benzene, citric acid, glucose, or fullerene are exposed to moderate temperature conditions (≥100 °C) and pressure (3–3.5 bar) within an autoclave, in most cases with a concentrated acid solution, leading to the formation of graphene's characteristic honeycomb structure [14–16].

Among the various synthesis methods of GQDs, pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) emerges as an auspicious and versatile approach. This technique relies on a purely physical mechanism, avoiding the need for high concentrations of chemical reagents, significantly reducing the process's environmental footprint. Additionally, PLAL operates efficiently under ambient temperature and pressure conditions, making it a practical and energy-efficient method. One of its key advantages is its ability to produce materials with exceptional purity, as the absence of harsh chemicals minimizes contamination and the introduction of undesired byproducts [17–19]. These attributes make PLAL an attractive choice for applications requiring high-purity nanomaterials, aligning well with sustainable development goals and advancing the field of green nanotechnology [17,20].

The PL emission of GQDs can span various spectral regions, including deep ultraviolet, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red bands [21–25]. Their reduced size and the types of chemical edge groups are the primary factors responsible for luminescence effects in metallic quantum dots; however, there is no consensus on the case of GQDs. It is believed that the dominant PL mechanism in GQDs involves either the quantum confinement effect of the π-conjugated domains, the effects of functional groups at the edges, or a synergistic effect between these two factors [26–29].

Despite the promising potential of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), significant gaps remain in understanding the photoluminescence properties of GQDs, particularly those produced via pulsed laser ablation in liquid. While PLAL minimizes environmental impact by avoiding chemical reagents and allowing synthesis under ambient conditions, challenges such as the effects of GQD aggregation in liquid mediums are still unaddressed. It is suggested that the core structure and functional groups at the edges of GQDs may play crucial roles in understanding both the aggregation process and photoluminescence behavior. In this context, this study explores the production of GQDs using the PLAL technique, employing a low-cost, user-friendly nanopulsed laser setup, with carbon black as the carbon source and ethanol as the liquid medium.

Carbon black (Vulcan XC72R) was donated by Cabot Corporation. Ethanol (purity >99.5 %) was obtained from Neon (Brazil). Sodium hydroxide (purity >99.0 %, Vetec) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, ACS reagent, 37 %) were used to prepare stock solutions at 2 mol L−1, which were subsequently diluted as needed. Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solutions were prepared using boric acid (H3BO3, 95 %, Vetec), acetic acid (CH3COOH, 95 %, Vetec), and phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 95 %, Vetec), to achieve a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1, with sodium hydroxide employed for pH adjustment. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (resistivity ∼18.2 MΩ cm) supplied by a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, Bedford, USA).

GQDs were prepared using a simple and eco-friendly PLAL method with carbon black as the carbon source in ethanol. Initially, 5 mg of carbon black was dispersed in 10 mL of ethanol (≥99,5 %, Neon) using an ultrasonic bath for 10 min to ensure a homogeneous suspension. Laser ablation was subsequently conducted on this carbon suspension for 2 h using a Super Mini Fiber Laser (Translaser®, Brazil) system at room temperature in an air environment. The ablation process employed a pulsed duration of 110 ns and a fundamental wavelength of 1064 nm, directly targeting the carbon suspension. Productivity was optimized by maintaining the laser fluence at 147.4 J cm−2 on the liquid surface throughout the ablation. Following ablation, the resulting yellow-orange solution was filtered through a 220 nm syringe filter and stored at 4 °C until further characterization.

UV–Vis spectra were collected in a Shimadzu UV−1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The measurements were conducted in a 10 mm wide quartz cell with a sample volume of 2.0 mL, covering a spectral range from 190 to 500 nm. Photoluminescence spectroscopy was performed in a bench spectrofluorometer (F-7000 FL Spectrophotometer). The excitation and emission wavelengths were recorded in the 220–500 and 230–700 nm ranges, respectively, with increments of 5 nm. Excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm. A quartz cuvette with a 1 cm optical path and four polished faces was used. A 90° angle geometry configuration between the excitation and emission was employed. All measurements were performed at room temperature. TEM analyses were conducted using a JEOL- JEM 2100 operated at a beam voltage of 200 kV. Sample preparation involved placing 10 μL of the colloidal solutions onto holey carbon-coated copper grids, followed by air drying. Raman spectra were measured in a dry sample at room temperature using a Renishaw Invia Raman microscope with 532 nm laser excitation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR in the range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer (ESCALAB™ XI+) featuring a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV), a 400 μm spot size, and a double-focusing hemispherical analyzer. Measurements were performed under an ultrahigh vacuum (<10−8 mBar) with charge compensation enabled via a flood gun. Survey spectra (10 scans per sample) were acquired at a pass energy of 200 eV, a step size of 1 eV, and a dwell time of 10 ms, while high-resolution C1s and O1s spectra (10 scans) utilized a pass energy of 20 eV, a step size of 0.025 eV, and a dwell time of 100 ms. Energy calibration referenced the adventitious carbon C1s peak at 284.8 eV, and spectral processing was performed using Thermo Advantage software (v5 9921) with Gaussian-Lorentzian fitting to resolve oxidation states. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were performed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument, which is equipped with a He-Ne laser operating at a wavelength of 633 nm. The measurements were taken at a fixed scattering angle of 173° and maintained at 25 °C, with ethanol serving as the solvent (refractive index n = 1.36). Additionally, zeta potential (ζ-potential) analyses were carried out using the same device. For these measurements, 750 μL of each sample was introduced into a disposable folded capillary cell.

To determine the morphology and distribution of the GQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were conducted. Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM image of the GQDs synthesized by PLAL, where a predominantly circular morphology is identified, along with a histogram created by counting at least 100 nanoparticles using ImageJ software. This revealed a size variation between approximately 1.0 and 7.5 nm, with a Gaussian fit curve and an average GQD size of about 4.0 nm, which is consistent with literature values [30–32].

UV–Vis analyses of the GQDs at concentrations ranging from 10 to 300 μg mL−1 were conducted to observe and identify the formation of several peaks in the 200–350 nm range, many of which are attributed to the presence of polyynes of different sizes, CnH2 [33]. The most intense polyyne peaks were observed at 200 (C6H2), 215 (C8H2), 225 (C8H2 and C10H2), and 251 (C10H2) nm, followed by bands at 238 (C10H2), 276 (C12H2), 305 (C14H2), and 325 (C16H2) nm, with lower intensity [33–35]. The contributions to absorbance can mostly be attributed to the polyynes; however, a significant portion comes from the GQDs, which can exhibit π→π∗ transition peaks of the sp2 hybridized domains of aromatic rings around 210 nm, n–π∗ transition peaks around 240 nm, and band-gap absorption peaks around 270 nm, as observed in Fig. 1(b) [36]. The absorption intensity increases linearly with concentration, as shown in Supporting Information in Fig. S1, reaching saturation at 100 μg mL−1.

Raman analyses were conducted to evaluate the presence and intensity of the D and G bands, which are associated with carbon materials featuring sp3 hybridization and defects in the crystalline structure, respectively. These defects are linked to the introduction of oxygenated groups, resulting in disruptions in the sp2 carbon networks. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the D peak appears at 1328 cm−1, and the G peak at 1572 cm−1. The ratio of these peaks can be related to the sp3/sp2 carbon proportion in the produced GQDs. The ID/IG ratio of 0.86 obtained for the material suggests a crystalline nature of the GQDs and an integrated graphite structure similar to that obtained in the literature [37–39]. FTIR analyses were conducted to observe the presence of functional groups within the material's structure, as shown in Fig. 1(d). The analysis revealed the presence of C-H carbon bonds with sp hybridization at 3273 cm−1, sp2 at 2859 cm−1, and sp3 at 2930 and 1378 cm−1 (CH3). Additionally, aromatic ring C=C stretching was observed at 1639 cm−1, aromatic out-of-plane bending at 804 cm−1, and oxygenated functional groups such as COOH and O-H at 3433 cm−1, C=O at 1714 cm−1, C-O-C at 1260 cm−1, and C-O at 1063 cm−1 [40,41]. The presence of oxygenated functional groups is one of the primary contributors to the PL phenomena in undoped GQDs and is also responsible for their high solubility in water [42,43]. The mass yield of GQD synthesis was determined by calculating the ratio of the dried GQD mass to the initial mass of Vulcan carbon consumed in the PLAL process. As shown in Table S2, approximately 61.5 % of the raw material was successfully converted into GQDs, while the remaining fraction was either removed in minor quantities through filtration, transformed into volatile byproducts such as carbon monoxide (CO) and carbon dioxide (CO2) [44], or carried away with ethanol during the drying stage. This result is consistent with previously reported values for laser-assisted GQD production, such as the study by Calabro et al. [30], which achieved a 53.7 % mass yield via the ablation of a solid pellet of carbon nano-onions. Despite its relevance, mass yield determination based on drying is rarely reported, as GQDs may be lost due to solvent evaporation. To optimize recovery, precise control of the drying conditions is crucial. Strategies such as slow evaporation under controlled temperatures, vacuum drying at low pressure, or solvent exchange with a less volatile liquid can minimize GQD loss. Additionally, centrifugation before drying can facilitate the separation of well-dispersed GQDs from the solvent, reducing unintentional losses. Further investigation into the influence of drying parameters on GQD retention could enhance synthesis efficiency and improve reproducibility in large-scale production.

GQDs are materials that exhibit PL and tend to act as excellent fluorophore reagents, with well-defined excitation and emission relationships that are correlated with the size of the produced material [28,42]. The quantum yield (QY) of the GQDs was measured relative to a Rhodamine B standard (Table S1). The synthesized GQDs exhibited an average QY of 5.3 %, which falls within the expected range for this synthesis method. According to the literature, QY values for GQDs typically range from 1 to 10 percent, depending on factors such as precursor composition, the method used, and post-synthesis modifications [45–47]. Although this QY is low compared to GQDs synthesized using hydrothermal or microwave-assisted methods, where QY values can exceed 10 percent, particularly with heteroatom doping [48,49], it demonstrates efficient photoluminescence without requiring additional chemical reagents. This suggests that the synthesized GQDs are suitable for applications in bioimaging and optoelectronics. Furthermore, optimizing the ablation conditions or incorporating functionalization strategies may further enhance their optical performance [47].

XPS was employed to analyze the chemical composition of GQDs, revealing distinct C1s and O1s peaks in the survey spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(b)) was deconvoluted into five Gaussian components centered at binding energies of 284.3, 285.0, 286.1, 287.2, and 288.5 eV. These peaks were attributed to graphitic sp2 hybridized carbon (C–C, C=C), sp3 hybridized carbon (C–C, C–O), hydroxyl groups (C–O), carbonyl groups (C=O), and carboxylic groups (O–C=O), respectively [30,32]. Similarly, the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) was resolved into three peaks located at 531.5, 532.2, and 533.3 eV, corresponding to carbonyl (C=O), hydroxyl (C–O), and carboxyl (C–OH/C–O–C) functionalities [50,51]. It has been reported that O–H groups exhibit greater stability compared to C=O and C–O=C groups in smaller GQDs. Consequently, smaller particle sizes are anticipated to result in an increased relative abundance of surface hydroxyl groups [41,52]. In the analyzed GQDs, hydroxyl-bonded carbons constitute the predominant oxygen-containing species, accounting for 62.5 % of the total oxygen-bonded carbons, while carbonyl and carboxylic carbons contribute 24.3 % and 13.2 %, respectively.

Hydroxyl groups on GQDs play a multifaceted role in governing both their physicochemical stability and optical performance. These groups, alongside carboxyl and epoxy functionalities, impart hydrophilicity to GQDs, enabling robust aqueous dispersibility through hydrogen bonding with water molecules [53]. Such interactions enhance water solubility and prevent aggregation, thereby preserving the structural and optical integrity of GQDs in aqueous environments [54]. Beyond solubility, hydroxyl groups critically modulate the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQDs. They induce a blue shift in emission spectra by localizing electronic states, which serve as radiative recombination centers [55]. Furthermore, hydroxyl-rich surfaces suppress nonradiative decay pathways, leading to heightened PL intensity and quantum yields [56]. This dual functionality, enhancing both colloidal stability and emissive behavior, highlights the pivotal role of hydroxyl functionalization in tailoring GQDs for applications ranging from bioimaging to environmental sensing, where aqueous compatibility and tunable optical properties are paramount.

Edge and size phenomena are commonly employed to theorize the emission range of GQDs. In this study, 2D mapping revealed that the photoluminescence profiles of these materials can also be influenced by concentration-related phenomena, leading to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and aggregation-induced emission. Comprehensive 2D and 3D PL analyses were performed to create an excitation-emission matrix over the range of λexc 220–500 nm and λemi 250–700 nm, utilizing the same concentration range as in the UV–Vis analyses. Tunable multicolored PL emissions induced by aggregation were identified through simple adjustments to the GQD concentration. Consistent with findings by Ozyurt et al. [50], photoluminescence quenching of GQDs was predominantly attributed to aggregation.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the maximum emission and the corresponding excitation spectra for A-GQDs (10 μg mL−1). The PL excitation spectra exhibit two well-defined peaks at ∼235 and 271 nm, along with four additional bands at ∼247, 312, 366, and 411 nm. The peaks at 235 and 271 nm correspond to π-π∗ transitions, where the 235 nm peak can be related to the electronic transition of double bonds present in the aromatic rings at the center of the A-GQDs, while the 271 nm band may be associated with C=O double bonds resulting from the edges of the A-GQDs in intermediate aggregates. The band at 366 nm is known to arise from n-π∗ transition interactions of molecular orbitals of oxygenated functional groups such as C=O, -COOH, and C-OH present at the material's edges [50,57]. The lower wavelength excitation peaks at ∼235 and 247 nm correspond to the carbon core (region 1) and are characteristic of the different sizes found in the solution. Possible transition effects of C=C groups present at the edges of A-GQDs are observed at 271 nm and 312 nm (region 2), while the bands at 366 and 411 nm result from a secondary aggregation process of the A-GQDs (region 3), attributed to aggregation states or surface defects [50,57,58]. The peaks at 235 and 271 nm are the primary contributors to the maximum emission of A-GQDs (280 nm), as supported by the 2D (Fig. 3(d)) and 3D (Fig. S2) excitation PL spectroscopy mappings at different emission wavelengths.

The first excitation peak (region 1) with the highest intensity should be considered the primary excitation wavelength for maximum emission, although the third peak at 271 nm also plays a role in the PL emission of A-GQDs. This is illustrated by the 2D map of the emission-excitation relationship within a specific region of the graph (λ exc 250–290 nm). The emission peaks are maximized at approximately 280 nm, with no variation across different excitation wavelengths. Typically, the emission wavelength of carbon-based fluorescent materials depends on the excitation wavelength, with PL emission peaks shifting to the red as the excitation wavelength increases. However, A-GQDs exhibited PL emission that is independent of excitation, indicating uniform size as observed in the TEM analyses shown in Fig. 1 and a consistent surface state across the domains sp2 [50].

In Fig. 3(b), compared to A-GQDs, B-GQDs (100 μg mL−1) show lower intensity in region 1, followed by increased intensity in regions 2 and 3 bands, with no excitation PL shift observed, but a redshift in PL emission (from 310 nm to 410 nm) attributed to aggregation effects of the A-GQDs. The primary PL excitation peak, observed at 366 nm in region 3, arises from surface state activation due to aggregate formation at elevated concentrations, making it the optimal excitation wavelength for achieving maximum PL emission. The observed redshift likely results from enhanced interactions among monomers within larger aggregates that form at these higher concentrations [59]. The synthesized monomers primarily range between ∼2.5 and 7.5 nm. With increasing concentration, larger monomers tend to aggregate more readily than smaller ones, creating two distinct size distributions: one comprising predominantly small monomers (region 1) and the other containing larger aggregates (region 2). This effect is corroborated by the total emission intensity comparison of B-GQDs, where a broad excitation range from 250 to 380 nm is associated with emissions from both monomers and aggregates, as depicted in the 2D excitation-emission map in Fig. 3(e).

The 2D emission mapping shows a broad emission region, with the emission maximum for excitation ranging from 270 to 370 nm, exhibiting a noticeable redshift compared to A-GQDs. The maximum emission for the redshift changed from 310 to 410 nm in B-GQDs, which indicates J-type aggregation [50,59]. The 3D emission mapping further reveals that the total emission intensity for A-GQDs is greater at shorter wavelengths when compared to B-GQDs, a difference attributed to AIQ among monomers and the reduced intensity of emissions from aggregates (Fig. S3). Emissions associated with monomers at an excitation wavelength (λ exc) of 250–290 nm diminish in B-GQDs relative to A-GQDs, which supports the presence of AIQ, a phenomenon commonly observed in carbon-based quantum dots [57]. Conversely, new redshifted peaks appearing within the excitation range of 270–370 nm suggest the presence of AIE (Fig. S2). Both AIQ and AIE phenomena are, therefore, present in B-GQDs, showcasing the adjustability of PL with concentration changes.

Fig. 3(c) displays the maximal emission and corresponding excitation spectra for C-GQDs (300 μg mL−1), where the intensity in region 2 further declines compared to B-GQDs. Here, the primary excitation wavelength in Region 3 shifts to 376 nm, with maximum emission recorded at 495 nm. This represents a redshift from 410 nm to 495 nm compared to B-GQDs, indicative of aggregation effects (Fig. 3(f)). The 3D emission mapping shows that B-GQDs maintain a higher emission intensity at shorter wavelengths compared to C-GQDs, likely due to AIQ effects among medium-sized particles and the reduced emission from aggregates (Fig. S4). Emissions at λ exc = 250–290 nm from monomers become absent, while emissions from aggregates within the 270–370 nm excitation range show increased intensity relative to B-GQDs, indicating AIQ [50,59]. However, additional redshifted peaks within the 320–430 nm range point to AIE. Consequently, both AIQ and AIE effects are observed in C-GQDs, demonstrating the adjustability of PL in response to increasing concentrations.

DLS and ζ-potential measurements were employed to evaluate the degree of agglomeration in GQDs produced by PLAL over a range of concentrations. These complementary techniques provided valuable insights into the particle size distributions, aggregation behavior, and underlying electrostatic interactions that contribute to colloidal stability. The DLS data presented in Fig. S5 indicate that the GQDs display a bimodal size distribution. At lower concentrations, specifically from 0.1 to 10 μg mL−1, a primary population with sizes approximately centered around 10–20 nm is likely representative of individual graphene quantum dots or small aggregates. Additionally, a secondary population ranging between 100 and 500 nm suggests the formation of larger aggregates at these concentrations. As the concentration increases (100–1000 μg mL−1), the predominant particle size appears to shift toward the 500–1000 nm range, indicating that aggregation becomes more pronounced [60]. This behavior highlights the concentration-dependent aggregation tendencies of the GQDs, which may influence their photophysical properties.

Parallel ζ-potential measurements (Fig. S6) reveal a concentration-dependent trend, with values increasing from approximately −58 mV at 0.1 μg mL−1 to near-neutral and slightly positive values (up to +6.5 mV at 1000 μg mL−1). According to DLVO theory, zeta potentials below ±30 mV may reflect a reduction in electrostatic repulsion, thereby favoring aggregation [61]. These observations are pertinent to the photophysical properties of GQDs, particularly regarding AIE and AIQ. At lower concentrations, the relatively stable colloidal conditions might facilitate AIE by restricting intramolecular motion within smaller aggregates. Conversely, the emergence of larger aggregates at higher concentrations could lead to AIQ due to enhanced non-radiative energy transfer [50,57]. Overall, these findings underscore the influence of particle concentration and aggregation on the optical behavior of GQDs and highlight the importance of precise control during synthesis.

PL excitation analyses at different wavelengths and concentrations were conducted, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Excitation at 470 nm provided valuable insights into the contributions of core and edge effects to the emission. The analyses revealed that the influence of edge and core effects is concentration-dependent, as illustrated in Fig. 4(b). As previously discussed, intermediate phenomena that may be attributed to energy transitions via gaps in edge functional groups can also occur [62].

Energy levels caused by size effects can also be excited in GQDs, although they are less pronounced at higher concentrations, as shown by the PL spectrum at λ exc 300 μg mL−1. Typically, due to the shorter lifetime and higher transition probability, PL emission from defects is dominant compared to size effects [62]. This is why blue emission is always considered much more intense at lower concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4(b), for GQDs at higher concentrations, a region of excitation with greater intensity is achieved (λ exc 366 nm), attributed to emission mechanisms from nO2P → π∗ of possible oxygenated groups (-COOH) at the edges of the GQDs [63].

Gan et al. [62] suggest that when an appropriate energy gap exists, energy transfer from the functional groups to the sp2 clusters is facilitated. This interaction promotes size-dependent emissions while concurrently boosting the excitation of the sp2 clusters, notably at an excitation wavelength of 366 nm. Consequently, by adjusting both the functional groups and particle size, the PL emission of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be effectively tailored.

An important approach to evaluating the behavior of GQDs is the analysis of pH variation, considering that the protonation and deprotonation of edge groups are essential to understanding the type of aggregation and its impact on fluorescence emission. Fig. 5 (a–i) presents the 2D excitation-emission matrix analyses for GQDs at 100 μg mL−1 in Britton-Robinson buffer solution (universal buffer). Two maxima regions can be observed in the matrix: one at λ exc = 275 nm and λ emi = 375 nm and another at λ exc = 360 nm and λ emi = 450 nm, corresponding to more acidic pH values. For pH values above 7.0, a redshift is observed, with the emission maximum at λ exc = 370 nm and λ emi = 470 nm, indicating more controlled aggregation characterized by a single maximum region.

The pH-dependent fluorescence and aggregation behavior of laser-ablated GQDs are governed by the interplay of edge functional groups and the sp2/sp3 carbon core, as revealed by XPS analysis. Hydroxyl (–OH, 62.5 %), carboxylic (–COOH, 13.2 %), and carbonyl (C=O, 24.3 %) groups dominate the GQD surface, collectively dictating colloidal stability and optical properties. In acidic media (pH < 5), protonation of –OH and –COOH reduces surface charge, enabling π-π stacking of aromatic cores and hydrogen bonding between protonated edge groups and C=O. This aggregation, facilitated by weakened electrostatic repulsion, forms fractal-like clusters that quench fluorescence via nonradiative energy transfer [64]. Conversely, in basic conditions (pH > 10), deprotonation of –OH to –O- and –COOH to –COO- generates strong negative surface charges, stabilizing dispersion through electrostatic repulsion [64]. These findings align with the excitation-emission matrix analyses shown in Fig. 5(a–i), where two maxima regions are observed at lower pH values (λexc = 275 nm, λemi = 375 nm; λexc = 360 nm, λemi = 450 nm), transitioning to a single redshifted maximum (λexc = 370 nm, λemi = 470 nm) at higher pH values, indicative of more controlled aggregation.

The influence of the dispersion medium on GQD fluorescence is evident from the variations in excitation-emission matrices. The aqueous buffer medium strongly affects fluorescence behavior, likely due to aggregation mechanisms. Therefore, to elucidate the effects of edge and core groups with pH variation on fluorescence mechanisms, PL excitation and emission analyses were conducted in solutions of HCl 1 mol L−1 (proton saturation), ultrapure water, and NaOH 1 mol L−1 (electron saturation). The GQD concentration was fixed at 100 μg mL−1 in all solutions. Fig. 6 (a–c) presents the PL spectra, while Fig. 6(d–f) shows the 2D excitation-emission matrix variations under the three conditions.

Highly protonated environments, such as HCl 1 mol L−1, promote greater aggregation, resulting in broad and poorly resolved fluorescence peaks due to rapid non-radiative electron-hole recombination (Fig. 6(a) and (d)). In neutral media, a redshift occurs with the emission maximum at λexc = 460 nm, likely associated with oxygenated core groups contributing more significantly to fluorescence than edge groups (Fig. 6(b) and (e)). In deprotonated media, such as NaOH 1 mol L−1, well-resolved PL excitation peaks emerge, with a clear peak at λexc = 225 nm corresponding to the GQD core, alongside minor shoulders between λexc = 240–350 nm attributed to edge groups (Fig. 6(c) and (f)). These observations underscore the critical role of edge groups in modulating fluorescence, particularly under varying pH conditions, while the sp2 core remains indispensable for intrinsic fluorescence but requires edge-group-derived charges to prevent quenching.

The fluorescence modulation arises from synergistic interactions between edge functional groups and the carbon core (Table 1 ). Protonated –OH in acidic media localizes excitons via hydrogen bonding, acting as radiative centers, but aggregation-induced quenching dominates. In contrast, deprotonated –O- and –COO- in basic conditions withdraw electron density from the sp2 core, altering electronic transitions and stabilizing excited states through conjugation with C=O. The sp2 domains contribute intrinsic fluorescence via quantum confinement, but their optical output depends critically on edge-group-mediated dispersion. Aggregation disrupts these domains, diminishing emission, while well-dispersed sp2 structures maintain radiative recombination. Notably, C=O groups play dual roles: in acidic pH, they stabilize aggregates via hydrogen bonding, while in basic pH, they amplify electron withdrawal and exciton stabilization, underscoring their versatile influence.

These findings underscore the critical distinction between edge-functional-group contributions and core-mediated effects in graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The XPS-derived predominance of hydroxyl groups (–OH, 62.5 %) directly correlates with pH-responsive behavior, while carbonyl (C=O) and carboxylic (–COOH) groups collectively regulate electron density redistribution and aggregation pathways. The sp2-hybridized carbon core serves as the foundation for intrinsic fluorescence but relies on edge-group-derived surface charges to mitigate aggregation-induced quenching. Future NMR investigations could elucidate site-specific protonation equilibria at edge functional groups, thereby refining the mechanistic model. Overall, this study demonstrates that precise engineering of edge functionalization is critical for optimizing GQDs in applications demanding pH-tunable emissions and colloidal stability, such as environmental sensors or bioimaging platforms.

In strongly acidic conditions, fluorescence quenching in GQDs arises from rapid non-radiative electron-hole recombination, reducing the excited-state population [65]. Under highly alkaline conditions, structural transformations, such as the conversion of lactone to quinone groups, can significantly alter fluorescence intensity and emission color [66]. Amino groups further enhance fluorescence by stabilizing the excited state through strong electron-withdrawing effects. Moreover, pH-induced changes can promote the formation of J-type aggregates, leading to altered electronic transitions and distinct fluorescence characteristics [67]. The tunable nature of these properties, influenced by factors like concentration and solvent polarity, highlights their potential in advancing materials science, optoelectronic devices, and sensor technologies.

Thus, aggregation and emission phenomena in undoped GQDs are strongly influenced by the concentration and pH of the dispersion medium. The synthesis of these nanomaterials should account for the fact that GQDs produced in acidic media tend to aggregate and exhibit fluorescence predominantly associated with edge groups, such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on their surface, as seen in hydrothermal synthesis routes [13,14]. The analyses demonstrate that the synthesis method and reaction medium are critical for tuning emission to specific regions of the wavelength spectrum. Fig. 7 illustrates the proposed aggregation mechanism based on concentration and pH variation.

The fluorescence emission wavelengths of GQDs can be adjusted according to their concentration and pH, with the aggregation state being a key factor influencing the maximum fluorescence emission. Furthermore, GQDs produced via laser ablation can serve as purified, dopant-free materials suitable for studies investigating the isolated impact of doping on GQD-based nanomaterials.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully synthesized via pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) using ethanol as the solvent and carbon black as the carbon source, with magnetic stirring. The GQDs exhibited extensive, multicolored PL that varied with concentration due to aggregation effects. At higher concentrations, a progressive redshift was observed in the maximum PL excitation band, shifting from 235 nm to 272 nm, and subsequently to 365 nm (A-GQDs to B-GQDs to C-GQDs), along with a corresponding redshift and quenching in the maximum PL emission band from 310 nm to 410 nm, and afterward to 495 nm. This study demonstrated AIQ effects in the blue region and AIE effects at longer wavelengths, both influenced by the aggregation state of the GQDs.

The photoluminescence behavior of GQDs was also strongly dependent on the pH of the dispersion medium. At acidic pH values, the GQDs exhibited dual emission maxima at λ exc = 275 nm/λ emi = 375 nm and λ exc = 360 nm/λ emi = 450 nm, corresponding to higher aggregation levels influenced by the protonation of edge groups. At pH values above 7, a single emission maximum emerged at λ exc = 370 nm/λ emi = 470 nm, indicative of controlled aggregation and contributions predominantly from core groups. Neutral pH resulted in a redshifted emission peak at λ exc = 460 nm, attributed to oxygenated groups in the GQD core. These results highlight the significant role of pH in modulating aggregation and fluorescence, emphasizing the tunability of GQD photoluminescence for specific applications.

Although many studies have reported on the photoluminescence of GQDs, variability in preparation methods and structural properties, often influenced by different reagents and carbon sources, complicates the interpretation of their photoluminescence behavior. The photoluminescence mechanism proposed in this study provides essential insights into the origins of GQD photoluminescence when synthesized without acidic chemical agents, which could otherwise alter the GQD structure through doping. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of photoluminescence mechanisms in pure, undoped GQDs and support the design and synthesis of high-purity GQDs for advanced applications.
